There is no fear for you, nor shall you grieve" (ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلا أَنْتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ) is a command from the People of the Heights to the believers to enter Paradise after affirming their state through the question. This is what the context (السياق) implies.

The view of some regarding the verse—that it implies a suppressed verb of saying, i.e., "It was said to them by the All-Beneficent: 'Enter Paradise! There is no fear for you regarding what lies in your future, nor shall you grieve over anything that might spoil your present,'" and that the deletion of "saying" is frequent in the Revelation and the speech of the pure Arabs when known from context—is refuted by the fact that the context does not support it, nor do the indicators (القرائن) point to it in any way, as previously explained. Just because it is permissible to estimate a "saying" in one place where its meaning is readily understood does not mean it is permissible in every instance. What context or indicator points to that here?

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of the Heights in the Quran)

The Heights (الأعراف) are not mentioned in the Quran except in these four verses of Surah al-A'raf (46–49). It has been concluded through an exhaustive investigation of the noble verses that it is one of the noble human stations (المقامات الكريمة) that will manifest on the Day of Resurrection. Allah, the Glorified, has depicted it as a veil (حجاب) between the two abodes—the Abode of Reward and the Abode of Punishment—which separates one from the other. The veil, by its nature, is outside the ruling of being one of the two sides, while simultaneously being connected to both. The veil has heights (أعراف), and upon the heights are men overseeing the people from the first and the last. They witness every soul among them in its specific station, despite the variation of their stations, degrees, and depths—from the highest of the high (أعلى عليين) to the lowest of the low (أسفل سافلين). They recognize each of them by the specific state and the deeds they performed. They have the right to speak to whomever they wish among them, to grant safety to whomever they wish, and to command entry into Paradise by the permission of Allah.

It is inferred from this that they have a standing (موقف) outside the two standings of felicity (السعادة)—which is salvation through righteous deeds—and wretchedness (الشقاوة)—which is perdition through evil deeds. Their station is higher than both stations together; therefore, it is a source of judgment and authority over both.

To understand this, you may consider what you find with kings and sources of authority. There is a group favored by their bounty and included in their mercy, drawing from the udder of felicity whatever their souls desire. Others are imprisoned in their dungeons, punished with their painful torment, surrounded by the humiliation of wretchedness from every side. These are two vessels: the vessel of felicity and the vessel of wretchedness. The two vessels are distinct and do not mix with a third vessel in which judgment is passed, the affairs of each are rectified, and their matters are organized. In this [third] vessel are servants who serve the throne by interacting with both sides, presenting bounties to the people of felicity and delivering punishments to the people of wretchedness; yet they are among the felicitous. Another group, beyond the servants and workers, are the managers of the affairs of all; they are the closest intermediaries to the throne, and they too are among the felicitous. Thus, felicity has levels in terms of being absolute or restricted.

It is not impossible for the King of the Day of Judgment to single out a people for His mercy, entering them into Paradise due to their good deeds and spreading His blessings over them, as He is the All-Forgiving, the Possessor of Great Bounty. He enters others into His Fire and the abode of His humiliation due to the evil deeds they performed, as He is the All-Mighty, the Possessor of Vengeance, Severe in Punishment, and Possessor of the Grasp. He permits a third group to mediate between Him and the two parties by executing His commands and judgments among them or issuing them upon them—by making felicitous those who are felicitous and wretched those who are wretched. For He is the One, the All-Paramount (الواحد القهار), who by His oneness subdues everything as He wills, through mediation, or by granting felicity or wretchedness. The Exalted has said: "To whom belongs the sovereignty today? To Allah, the One, the All-Paramount" (40:16). So understand.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنادى أَصْحابُ النَّارِ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ أَنْ أَفِيضُوا** — "The inmates of the Fire will call out to the inhabitants of Paradise, ‘Pour..." (7:50) etc. *Ifadah* (الإفاضة) is from *fayd* (الفيض), which is the flowing of water as it is poured. The Exalted said: "You see their eyes overflowing with tears" (5:83), meaning their tears flow as they are poured. The conjunction of the rest of what Allah provided them of bounties with "water" indicates that *ifadah* refers to the pouring of bounties in general, whether liquid or otherwise, by way of a general metaphor (عموم المجاز). It is also said that *ifadah* is literally the giving of abundant bounty, in which case its application to water and other things would be literal.

In any case, the verse contains a suggestion of the high status of the people of Paradise relative to the place of the people of the Fire.

Water was singled out—though it is among what Allah provided them—and then mentioned before the rest of what Allah provided them because the need for cool water naturally occurs to the mind before other things when heat surrounds a person. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَهُمْ لَهْواً وَلَعِباً** — "Those who took their religion for a diversion and play..." (7:51) to the end of the verse. Diversion (اللهو) is that which distracts you from what is important to you. Play (اللعب) is an action performed for an imaginary, non-real end. Delusion (الغرور) is the outward display of sincerity while concealing deception. Forgetfulness (النسيان) is the opposite of remembrance (الذكر); it is sometimes used metaphorically for abandoning a thing and not caring for its affair, like a forgotten thing, and it follows this sense in the verse. Defiance (الجحد) is negation and denial. The verse is intended to define the disbelievers, and three definitions of disbelief (الكفر) can be derived from it: first, that it is a person's taking his religion as diversion and play and being deluded by the life of this world; second, the forgetting of the Day of Meeting; and third, the defiance of the signs of Allah. Each of these definitions has a basis.

In His saying, the Exalted: "Those who took their religion for a diversion and play," there is an indication that a human being cannot do without religion in any case—even those occupied with diversion and play who have completely immersed their lives in them. For religion (الدين)—as previously alluded to in the commentary on His saying: "who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked" (7:45)—is the path of life that a human being treads in the world, and he has no choice but to tread it. Allah, the Glorified, has organized it according to what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) guides to and calls for; it is the religion of the human being that belongs to him and is attributed to him, and it is what concerns the human being and drives him toward a real end, which is the felicity of his life.

So, whenever a human being proceeds upon it and treads it, he is upon his religion, which is the primordial religion of Allah. But whenever he turns away from it to something else that distracts him and does not guide him except to imaginary ends—which are material pleasures that have no permanence and no benefit that returns to his felicity—he has taken his religion as diversion and play, and the life of this world has deluded him with the mirage of its ornaments.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** **فَالْيَوْمَ نَنْساهُمْ كَما نَسُوا لِقاءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هذا** — "So today We will forget them just as they forgot the encounter of this day of theirs" (7:51). That is: Today We abandon them and do not provide the necessities of their felicitous life, just as they abandoned this day of theirs and did not perform what was necessary to work for it, "and as they used to impugn Our signs" (7:51). Similar to this verse in making the denial of the signs a cause for Allah's forgetting him on the Day of Resurrection is His saying: "He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today’" (20:126), where defiance was replaced with forgetfulness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ جِئْناهُمْ بِكِتابٍ فَصَّلْناهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ** — "Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge..." (7:52). This verse is a return to the beginning of the discourse, namely His saying at the start of the verses: "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs?" (7:37). That is: Who is more unjust than these, when We have completed the proof against them and established the exposition for them, bringing them a Book that We elaborated and sent down to them based on Our knowledge of its descent?

So His saying: "with knowledge" (عَلى عِلْمٍ) is connected to His saying "We have brought them" (لَقَدْ جِئْناهُمْ). The phrase contains an argument for the truthfulness of the Book. The implied meaning is: We have brought them a Book of truth; and how could it not be truth when it was sent down based on Our knowledge of the subjects it contains?

And His saying: "a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith" (هُدىً وَرَحْمَةً لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ). That is: a guidance and a showing of the path for all, and a mercy specifically for those who believe in it. Or, a guidance and a bringing to the objective for the believers, and a mercy for them. The first is more suitable to the context, which is a context of argumentation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ** — "Do they await anything but its interpretation?" (7:53) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "its interpretation" (تأويله) refers to the Book. It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses..." (3:7), that interpretation (التأويل) in the usage of the Quran is the reality (الحقيقة) upon which a ruling, a report, or any other apparent matter relies, as the manifest relies on the hidden and the parable on the reality.

So His saying: "Do they await anything but its interpretation?" means: Do these who fabricate lies against Allah or deny His signs—when the proof has been completed against them by the Quran sent down to them—await anything but the reality of the matter which was the motive for the course of its expositions, the legislation of its rulings, and the warning and good tidings within it? For if they did not await it, they would not have abandoned following what is in it.

Then He, the Exalted, informs of their state on the day the interpretation arrives, saying: "The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it..." etc. That is: When the reality of the matter is uncovered on the Day of Resurrection, those who abandoned it will confess to the truth of what the Messengers brought of the laws they were obligated to act upon, and their report that Allah would resurrect them and recompense them for them.

When they witness then that their hands are empty of good and that they are perishing due to the corruption of their deeds, they will ask for one of two things to rectify what has been corrupted of their affair: either intercessors (شفعاء) to save them from the destruction that looms over them, or themselves, by being returned to the world to perform righteous deeds other than the evil ones they used to do. That is His saying, narrating from them: "‘Are there any intercessors to intercede for us? Or can we be sent back, so that we may act differently from what we used to act?’" (7:53).

And His saying, the Exalted: "They have certainly ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them" (7:53). This is a separate sentence serving as a justification for what was narrated from them regarding their request for one of the two things: either intercessors or a return to the world. It is as if it were asked: Why do they ask for what they ask? It was said: "They have certainly ruined their souls" by exchanging their religion for diversion and play, and choosing defiance over submission. The deluding fabrications that used to veil them from this in the world have vanished from them, so it became clear to them that they are in need of someone to rectify their deeds for them—either themselves or others who might intercede for them.

It has already been mentioned in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة) in the first volume of this book that in His saying: "Are there any intercessors to intercede for us?" there is an indication that there are indeed intercessors who intercede for the people, as He said "any intercessors" (مِنْ شُفَعاءَ) and did not say "any intercessor" (من شفيع).

## Narratival Discourse

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَما أَضَلَّنا إِلَّا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — "and none led us astray except the guilty" (26:99): "When they called them to their path—that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding them when He gathers them to the Fire: **قالَتْ أُخْراهُمْ لِأُولاهُمْ رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا فَآتِهِمْ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً مِنَ النَّارِ** — 'The last of them will say about the first of them, "Our Lord, it was these who led us astray; so give them a double punishment of the Fire"' (7:38)." And His saying: **كُلَّما دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَعَنَتْ أُخْتَها** — "Every time an ummah enters, it will curse its sister-ummah" (7:38)—until they are all gathered therein, they disavow one another and curse one another. He means that some of them will argue against others hoping for victory to escape the greatness of what has descended upon them, but it is not a time of trial or testing, nor of the acceptance of excuses, nor a time of salvation.

I say: His saying (peace be upon him) "His saying: 'Every time an ummah enters...'" etc., is a narration of the verse by its meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **لا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوابُ السَّماءِ** — "the gates of heaven will not be opened for them" (7:40), Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: 'The gates of heaven will not be opened for them' (لا يفتح لهم) with the letter *ya*."

In the same source, al-Tayalisi, Ibn Shaybah, Ahmad, Hannad ibn al-Sari, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud in his *Sunan*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in the book *‘Adhab al-Qabr* narrated from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for the funeral of a man from the Ansar. We reached the grave before the niche (اللحد) was prepared. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sat down and we sat around him as if birds were upon our heads. In his hand was a stick with which he was scratching the ground. He raised his head and said: 'Seek refuge from the punishment of the grave' two or three times.

Then he said: 'When the believing servant is at the point of departure from the world and arrival at the Hereafter, angels with white faces descend to him from heaven, as if their faces were the sun. With them are shrouds from the shrouds of Paradise and embalming scents (حنوط) from the scents of Paradise. They sit from him at the distance of the eyesight. Then the Angel of Death comes and sits at his head and says: "O good soul, come out to forgiveness from Allah and His pleasure." It comes out flowing as a drop flows from the mouth of a water-skin—though you may see otherwise—and he takes it. When he takes it, they do not leave it in his hand for the blink of an eye until they take it and place it in that shroud and that embalming scent. There comes from it a fragrance like the finest scent of musk found on the face of the earth. They ascend with it, and they do not pass by a group of angels except that they say: "What is this good spirit?" They reply: "So-and-so, the son of so-and-so," using the best names they used to call him in the world, until they reach the lowest heaven. They ask for it to be opened for him and it is opened for them. Those brought near (مقربوها) from every heaven accompany him to the next heaven until he reaches the seventh heaven. Then Allah says: "Write the book of My servant in *‘Illiyyin* and return him to the earth, for from it I created them, into it I return them, and from it I shall bring them out another time." So his spirit is returned to his body.

Two angels come to him, sit him up, and say to him: "Who is your Lord?" He says: "My Lord is Allah." They say to him: "What is your religion?" He says: "My religion is Islam." They say to him: "Who is this man who was sent among you?" He says: "He is the Messenger of Allah." They say to him: "What is your knowledge?" He says: "I read the Book of Allah, believed in it, and confirmed it." Then a caller calls out from heaven: "My servant has spoken the truth. So spread for him [carpets] from Paradise, clothe him from Paradise, and open for him a gate to Paradise." Its breeze and fragrance come to him, his grave is widened for him as far as his eye can see, and a man with a handsome face, fine clothes, and a pleasant scent comes to him and says: "Rejoice in that which pleases you; this is your day which you were promised!" He says to him: "Who are you? Your face is a face that brings good." He says: "I am your righteous deeds." He says: "My Lord, establish the Hour! Establish the Hour, so that I may return to my family and my wealth."

He said: 'And when the disbelieving servant is at the point of arrival at the Hereafter and departure from the world, angels with black faces descend to him from heaven with hair-cloth (المسوح). They sit from him at the distance of the eyesight. Then the Angel of Death comes and sits at his head and says: "O wicked soul, come out to the wrath of Allah and His anger." It scatters throughout his body and he pulls it out as a multi-pronged spit (السفود) is pulled out from wet wool, and he takes it.

When he takes it, they do not leave it in his hand for the blink of an eye until they place it in that hair-cloth. There comes from it a stench like the foulest smell of a carcass found on the face of the earth. They ascend with it, and they do not pass by a group of angels except that they say: "What is this wicked spirit?" They reply: "So-and-so, the son of so-and-so," using the ugliest names he was called in the world, until they reach the lowest heaven. They ask for it to be opened, but it is not opened for him.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: **لا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوابُ السَّماءِ** — 'the gates of heaven will not be opened for them' (7:40).

'Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: "Write his book in *Sijjin* in the lowest earth." His spirit is then flung down.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited: **مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَكَأَنَّما خَرَّ مِنَ السَّماءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ الطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِي بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي مَكانٍ سَحِيقٍ** — 'Whoever ascribes partners to Allah, it is as if he has fallen from the sky, and the birds snatch him, or the wind blows him to a remote place' (22:31).

'His spirit is returned to his body. Two angels come to him, sit him up, and say to him: "Who is your Lord?" He says: "Ha! Ha!" They say to him: "What is your religion?" He says: "Ha! Ha! I do not know!" They say to him: "Who is this man who was sent among you?" He says: "Ha! Ha! I do not know!" Then a caller calls out from heaven: "My servant has lied. So spread for him [carpets] from the Fire and open for him a gate to the Fire." Its heat and scorching wind come to him, and his grave is tightened upon him until his ribs interlock.

A man with an ugly face, foul clothes, and a putrid scent comes to him and says: "Receive news of that which will grieve you; this is your day which you were promised." He says: "Who are you? Your face is a face that brings evil." He says: "I am your wicked deeds." He says: "My Lord, do not establish the Hour..."'"

I say: This narration is among the famous ones, narrated by a group of authors in their books as you have seen. Regarding its meaning, there are narrations from the paths of the Shiah from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), some of which we included in the narratival discourse placed under the saying of the Exalted: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ** — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way 'dead'..." (2:154) in the first volume of this book.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Sa'id ibn Janah, who said: ‘Awf ibn ‘Abdullah al-Azdi narrated to me from Jabir ibn Yazid al-Ju'fi, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), in a tradition regarding the seizing of the disbeliever's soul: "When his spirit is brought to the lowest heaven, the gates of heaven are closed to it. That is His saying: 'the gates of heaven will not be opened for them' to the end of the verse. Allah says: 'Return it to him, for from it We created you, into it We return you, and from it We shall bring you out another time.'"

I say: Something similar was narrated in *al-Majma‘* from him (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Aishah that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this verse: **لَهُمْ مِنْ جَهَنَّمَ مِهادٌ وَمِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ غَواشٍ** — "They shall have a bed of hell, and sheets [of fire] above them" (7:41). He said: "They are layers above him and layers beneath him; he does not know if what is above him is greater or what is beneath him. However, the lower layers raise him and the upper layers press him down, and it tightens upon them until he is like the ferrule (الزج) in a cup."

In the same source, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) who said: "By Allah, regarding us, the People of Badr, this verse was sent down: **وَنَزَعْنا ما فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنْ غِلٍّ** — 'We will remove whatever rancor is in their breasts' (7:43)."

I say: The occurrence of this sentence in the context of these verses, which are Meccan, precludes its descent on the Day of Badr or regarding the People of Badr. This sentence also occurs in the saying of the Exalted: **وَنَزَعْنا ما فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنْ غِلٍّ إِخْواناً عَلى سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ** — "We will remove whatever rancor is in their breasts, [and they will be] brothers, [reclining] on couches, facing one another" (15:47), which is also in the context of verses describing the people of Paradise and is Meccan.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Hasan who said: "It reached me that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The people of Paradise will be detained after they cross the Bridge (الصراط) until grievances (ظلاماتهم) they had against one another in the world are settled. Then they will enter Paradise with no rancor in the hearts of any of them toward another.'"

In the same source, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Abi al-Dunya, Ibn Jarir in *Dhikr al-Mawt*, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Every inhabitant of the Fire will see his place in Paradise and say: "If only Allah had guided us," and it will be a source of regret for them. And every inhabitant of Paradise will see his place in the Fire and say: "Had it not been that Allah guided us," and that is their gratitude.'"

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Darimi, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah and Abu Sa'id from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding: **وَنُودُوا أَنْ تِلْكُمُ الْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوها بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "And they will be addressed, ‘This is the Paradise you have been given to inherit for what you used to do’" (7:43). He said: "They will be addressed: 'Be healthy and never fall ill; be in bliss and never despair; be young and never grow old; and live forever and never die.'"

I say: Regarding the meaning of inheriting Paradise, there are other narrations that will follow, Allah willing.

In *al-Kafi* and *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with their chains to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ** — "Then a caller will call out among them, ‘The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers’" (7:44). He said: "The caller (المؤذن) is the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him)."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it from him (peace be upon him), and it was narrated in *Rawdat al-Wa'izin* from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) who said: "The caller is ‘Ali (peace be upon him)."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain to Jabir al-Ju'fi, from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "The Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), delivered a sermon in Kufa upon his return from Nahrawan. It reached him that Mu'awiyah was reviling him, disparaging him, and killing his companions. So he stood to deliver a sermon..." He mentioned the sermon until he said in it: "And I am the caller (المؤذن) in this world and the Hereafter. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Then a caller will call out among them, "The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers."' I am that caller. And He said: **وَأَذانٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** — 'An announcement from Allah and His Messenger' (9:3). I am that announcement (الأذان)."

I say: That is, "I am the caller with that announcement," by the evidence of the beginning of the speech. He (peace be upon him) is referring to the story of the verses of Disavowal (براءة).

In *al-Majma‘*, al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated with his chain to Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah from ‘Ali that he said: "I am that caller."

And with his chain to Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas that he said: "For ‘Ali, there are names in the Book of Allah that the people do not recognize. His saying: 'Then a caller will call out among them'—he says: 'Behold, the curse of Allah be upon those who denied my guardianship (ولايتي) and belittled my right.'"

I say: Al-Alusi said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* regarding the saying of the Exalted: "Then a caller will call out..." etc.: "According to what is narrated from Ibn Abbas, he is the Angel of the Trumpet (صاحب الصور). It is also said he is Malik, the keeper of the Fire. It is also said he is an angel other than them whom Allah commands to do so. The narration of the Imamiyyah from al-Rida and Ibn Abbas—that it is ‘Ali, may Allah ennoble his face—is something not established through the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah, and it is far-fetched for this Imam to be a caller while he is then in the enclosures of holiness (حظائر القدس)." (End of quote).

The author of *al-Manar* said in his *Tafsir* after quoting him: "I say: Those who compiled the books of *al-Jarh wa al-Ta'dil* (evaluation of narrators) did not base them on the principles of sectarian schools. Among their leading scholars were those counted among the Shiah of ‘Ali and his family, such as ‘Abd al-Razzaq and al-Hakim. None of them failed to declare many Shiah as reliable in their narrations. So if this narration is established with a sound chain, we accept it. We do not see his being in the enclosures of holiness as an impediment to it. If we could conceive a meaning for attributing this calling to him—may Allah ennoble his face—by which it would be considered a virtue or a reward with Allah, we would accept the narration even with less than a sound chain, as long as it is not fabricated or contradicted by a narration stronger in chain or sounder in text." (End of quote).

He did well in what he stated. However, solitary narrations (الآحاد) are not a proof (حجة) for us unless they are surrounded by indicators that provide knowledge—meaning complete personal confidence—whether they concern the fundamentals of religion, history, virtues, or otherwise. Except in jurisprudence (الفقه), where general confidence (الوثوق النوعي) is sufficient for the authority of a narration. All of this is provided it does not contradict the Book. The detail is left to the science of the Principles of Jurisprudence (أصول الفقه).

As for this calling being a virtue, there should be no doubt about it. Let the Hereafter's calling be measured by the worldly calling. Calling (التأذين) is the announcement of a judgment by its owner so that it may be established upon those judged. The caller is the link (الرابطة) connecting the owner of the judgment to those judged by affirming His judgment upon them. The link follows the two sides in its nobility or baseness. It is clear that if the side is Allah, exalted be His Name, there is in that a nobility and honor that nothing equals—as in the mediation of Ibrahim from Allah, the Glorified, in His saying: "And proclaim the Hajj to the people" (22:27), and the mediation of ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in conveying the verses of Disavowal: "An announcement from Allah and His Messenger to the people..." (9:3). This is in the legislative calling and announcement by which the ruler's judgment is established upon those subject to it. As for the non-legislative calling, such as the calling on the Day of Resurrection that the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers, it involves the establishment of complete distance and absolute, permanent cursing upon the wrongdoers after they have witnessed the truth of the divine promise that reached them from Him, the Exalted, through His prophets and messengers. It contains the consolidation of the result returning to the wrongdoers from the manifestation of the realities of the promise and the threat. So understand that, and do not belittle the matter of realities, nor be negligent in investigating them if you have a firm footing therein.

This is what ‘Ali (peace be upon him) himself alluded to in his previously mentioned sermon when he said: "And I am the caller in this world and the Hereafter."

The narration—as mentioned—is reported through multiple paths from the Shiah from ‘Ali, al-Baqir, and al-Rida (peace be upon them). From the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah is what al-Hakim narrated with his chain from Ibn al-Hanafiyyah from ‘Ali, and with his chain from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas. The man [Abu Salih] is good in narration and precise in hadith; his narrations in exegesis are transmitted in narratival commentaries and elsewhere. Yet they did not mention this narration of his; even al-Suyuti, who is exhaustive in *al-Durr al-Manthur* regarding what is narrated in exegesis, omitted the mention of this hadith. I do not know what the reason for that is.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The Scale (الميزان) will be placed on the Day of Resurrection and good deeds and evil deeds will be weighed. Whoever's good deeds outweigh his evil deeds by the weight of a nit (صؤابة), he enters Paradise. And whoever's evil deeds outweigh his good deeds by the weight of a nit, he enters the Fire.'

It was asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, what about those whose good deeds and evil deeds are equal?' He said: 'Those are the People of the Heights—they have not entered it, though they hope to.'"

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Abu Zur'ah ibn ‘Amr ibn Jarir who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked about the People of the Heights. He said: 'They are the last of the servants to be judged. When the Lord of the Worlds finishes judging between the servants, He says: "You are a people whom your good deeds saved from the Fire but you did not enter Paradise. You are My emancipated ones; graze in Paradise wherever you wish."'"

I say: The view that the People of the Heights are those whose good deeds and evil deeds are equal was narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, Hudhayfah, and Ibn Abbas among the Companions.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Hamzah al-Tayyar, who said: "Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) said: 'The people are of six categories...'—until he said—I asked: 'And what are the People of the Heights?' He said: 'A people whose good deeds and evil deeds are equal. If He enters them into the Fire, it is because of their sins; and if He enters them into Paradise, it is by His mercy.'" (The hadith).

In the same source, with its chain to Zurarah, who said: "Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'What do you say regarding the People of the Heights?' I said: 'They are either believers or disbelievers. If they enter Paradise, they are believers; and if they enter the Fire, they are disbelievers.' He said: 'By Allah, they are neither believers nor disbelievers. If they were believers, they would have entered Paradise as the believers entered it. If they were disbelievers, they would have entered the Fire as the disbelievers entered it. Rather, they are a people whose good deeds and evil deeds are equal, so their deeds fell short for them, and they are as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said.'

I asked: 'Are they from the people of Paradise or the people of the Fire?' He said: 'Leave them as Allah left them.' I said: 'Shall I defer them (أرجئهم)?' He said: 'Yes, defer them as Allah deferred them. If He wills, He will enter them into Paradise by His mercy; and if He wills, He will drive them to the Fire because of their sins, and He has not wronged them.' I asked: 'Does a disbeliever enter Paradise?' He said: 'No.' I asked: 'Does anyone enter the Fire except a disbeliever?' He said: 'No, unless Allah wills. O Zurarah, I say: Whatever Allah wills. Indeed, if you grow older, you will return and your knots will be loosened for you.'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him) "if you grow older..." etc., means: If you find my statement too great to accept, you will depart from the truth you were upon, and what you tied your heart upon of confirmation will be undone.

The narrations—as you see—interpret the People of the Heights as those whose good deeds and evil deeds are equal in the Scale. In some of them, His saying, the Exalted: "They have not entered it, though they hope to" etc., is from their speech. This does not correspond to the verses of the Heights at all, as previously explained.

Furthermore, you have already learned in the preceding commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "The weighing on that day is a truth..." (7:8) etc., that the Scale He mentions either becomes heavy—which is the preponderance of good deeds—or light—which is the preponderance of evil deeds. There is no meaning then for the equality of good and evil deeds, which would be the heaviness and lightness of the Scale simultaneously! Even if we assume there is someone whose Scale does not indicate the preponderance of some of his deeds over others, he would be among those for whom no weight is established on the Day of Resurrection—like the disbeliever whose deeds were annulled (أحبطت), or the marginalized (المستضعف) against whom the proof was not completed and upon whom no obligation was placed.

Yes, it might be inferred from the last narration that the intended meaning of those whose good and evil deeds are equal is the marginalized who are deferred to the command of Allah—if He wills He forgives them, and if He wills He punishes them. Thus, equality is a metaphor for the lack of preponderance, and the problem of the weighing is then resolved. However, the problem remains regarding the correspondence to the manifest meaning of the verses, which contain attributes of the men of the Heights and its people that can only be attributed to the Foremost (السابقون), the Proximate (المقربون), who preside in the enclosure of honor and felicity. These marginalized ones, if it is correct to count them among the people of felicity, are at its lowest levels.

In *al-Majma‘*, Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) said: "The Heights are dunes between Paradise and the Fire. Every Prophet and every Successor (خليفة) will be stationed upon them with the sinners of his time—just as the commander of an army stands with the weak of his soldiers—while the righteous have already preceded to Paradise. That Successor will say to the sinners standing with him: 'Look at your righteous brothers who have preceded.' So the sinners will greet them with peace; that is His saying: 'And they called out to the inhabitants of Paradise: "Peace be upon you!"' Then He, the Glorified and Exalted, informed that they had not entered it though they hoped to—meaning these sinners had not entered Paradise, yet they hoped that Allah would enter them through the intercession of the Prophet and the Imam. These sinners look at the people of the Fire and say: 'Our Lord, do not put us with the wrongdoing people.'

Then the People of the Heights—who are the Prophets and the Successors—will call out to certain men from the people of the Fire, rebuking them: 'Your amassing did not avail you, nor what you used to wax proud about. Are these the ones of whom you swore'—meaning these marginalized ones whom you used to deem weak and despise for their poverty, while you acted haughtily toward them with your worldly life—then they say to these marginalized ones, by a command from Allah to them: 'Enter Paradise! There is no fear for you, nor shall you grieve.'"

I say: Al-Qummi narrated in his *Tafsir* from his father, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Abi Ayyub, from Marthad, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), something close to this.

This narration—as you see—explicitly mentions the marginalized in place of those whose good and evil deeds are equal. Then it mentions that there is a group of the marginalized who hope to enter Paradise and seek refuge from entering the Fire, without interpreting the "men" whom Allah mentioned are on the Heights, recognizing each by their marks and calling them the People of the Heights, as being them. It then becomes easy to harmonize its content with the verses. No problem remains except the apparent sense of the verses that those who greet the people of Paradise are the People of the Heights and the men who are on the Heights.

It appears that there is a discrepancy in the narrations, arising from the misunderstanding of some transmitters followed by their transmission. Perhaps what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or some of the Imams explained was that there is a group of the marginalized whom Allah enters into Paradise through intercession or will, then the transmission by meaning altered it and brought it out in the form you see. This is apparent, like the other narrations reported from Ibn Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud, Hudhayfah, and others, saying that the men on the Heights are those whose good and evil deeds are equal, despite the differences in their texts. Likewise, the narration of al-Qummi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him); refer to it and you will recognize the truth of what we have claimed.

In *al-Basa’ir*, with its chain to Jabir ibn Yazid, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the Heights—what are they? He said: 'They are the most honored of creation before Allah, the Blessed and Exalted.'"

I say: The questioner takes the Heights and the men upon it as one, and the answer from him (peace be upon him) was based on that. It is as if he took it as a plural of *‘urf* (عرف) in the sense of an overseer (عريف) or a knower (عارف). Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations, some of which follow.

In the same source, with its chain to Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) regarding: **وَعَلَى الْأَعْرافِ رِجالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّا بِسِيماهُمْ** — "And on the Heights are men who recognize each by their marks" (7:46). He said: "We are the People of the Heights. Whoever recognizes us, his destination is Paradise; and whoever denies us, his destination is the Fire."

I say: His saying "Whoever recognizes us and whoever denies us"—if it is a verb and a subject, then so be it. If it is a verb and an object, it would be on the pattern of other narrations: "Whoever recognizes them and they recognize him, and whoever denies them and they deny him."

In the same source, with its chain to al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: "I was with the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) when a man said to him: 'And on the Heights are men who recognize each by their marks.' ‘Ali (peace be upon him) said to him: 'We are the Heights (الأعراف); we recognize our supporters by their marks. We are the Heights through whose path of recognition alone Allah is recognized. We are the Heights who will be stationed on the Day of Resurrection between Paradise and the Fire. None shall enter Paradise except he who recognizes us and we recognize him, and none shall enter the Fire except he who denies us and we deny him. That is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.

Had He willed, He would have made Himself known to the people so they would recognize His limit and come to Him through His gate. He made us His gates, His path (صراطه), His way (سبيله), and His gate through which He is approached.'"

I say: It was also narrated with its chain from Muqarrin from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him). The man questioning was Ibn al-Kawwa’. Al-Kulayni also narrated this story in *al-Kafi* from Muqarrin, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) saying: 'Ibn al-Kawwa’ came...'" etc.

It appears that the intended meaning of recognition (المعرفة) and denial (الإنكار) in the narration is recognition through love and hatred. That is: None shall enter Paradise except he who recognizes us through guardianship (الولاية) and we recognize him through obedience; and none shall enter the Fire except he who denies our guardianship and we deny his obedience. This is different from their recognizing everyone by their persons; otherwise, it would be difficult to harmonize it with His saying, the Exalted: "men who recognize each by their marks" and His saying, the Exalted: "And the People of the Heights called out to men whom they recognized by their marks" etc. Perhaps this arose from some narrators transmitting the narration by its meaning. What we have inferred is supported by what follows in the next narration.

In *al-Majma‘*, al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani narrated with his chain, raising it to al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: "I was sitting with ‘Ali (peace be upon him) when Ibn al-Kawwa’ came to him and asked him about this verse. He said: 'Woe to you, O Ibn al-Kawwa’! We shall be stationed on the Day of Resurrection between Paradise and the Fire. Whoever supported us, we shall recognize him by his mark and enter him into Paradise; and whoever hated us, we shall recognize him by his mark and enter him into the Fire.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Hilqam, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: 'And on the Heights are men who recognize each by their marks.' What does He mean by His saying: 'On the Heights are men'? He said: 'Do you not recognize that you have overseers (عرفاء) over your tribes, to know who among them is righteous or wicked?' I said: 'Yes.' He said: 'We are those men who recognize each by their marks.'"

I say: This is based on taking "the Heights" (الأعراف) as a plural of *‘urf* (العرف), like *aqtab* is the plural of *qutb*. *‘Urf* is the known matter; perhaps it is a verbal noun in the sense of the object. Thus, the meaning of "And on the Heights are men" is: And over their affairs and conditions known from them are men entrusted. This does not contradict what was previously mentioned that the Heights are the summits of the veil, nor what was mentioned in some narrations that the Heights are dunes between Paradise and the Fire. For recognition (المعرفة), which is the root of the word, preserves its meaning in its derivatives and instances of usage in any case.

Know that the reports from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding meanings close to these concerning the Heights are very numerous. What we have cited to indicate the types of their contents in interpreting the Heights and the People of the Heights is sufficient.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Tha'labi in his *Tafsir*, from Ibn Abbas, that he said: "The Heights is an elevated place on the Bridge (الصراط), upon which are al-Abbas, Hamzah, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, and Ja'far the Two-Winged. They recognize their Shiah by the whiteness of their faces, and those who hate them by the blackness of their faces."

I say: The transmission of the narration from *Majma‘ al-Bayan*, from the *Tafsir* of al-Tha'labi, from al-Dahhak, from Ibn Abbas, has already preceded in the previous exposition.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Harith ibn Abi Usamah narrated in his *Musnad*, and Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh from ‘Abdullah ibn Malik al-Hilali, from his father, who said: "Someone said: 'O Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), what are the People of the Heights?' He said: 'They are a people who went out in the way of Allah without the permission of their fathers. They were martyred, so their martyrdom prevented them from entering the Fire, and their disobedience to their fathers prevented them from entering Paradise. They are the last to enter Paradise.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated through other paths from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Abu Hurayrah, and Ibn Abbas. The problem with it has already been mentioned: its lack of correspondence to the manifest sense of the verses. Established principles dictate that if going out [for Jihad] was an individual obligation (وجوبا عينيا), the lack of parental permission would not affect it. If it was not an individual obligation but remained a collective one (على الكفاية), then going out would be prohibited, and being killed in battle would not benefit him unless he was marginalized (مستضعفا) due to ignorance of the ruling. This would then return to the view that the People of the Heights are the marginalized, and the previous discussion would apply to it.
